questions.question:
Which of the following correctly identifies all four parts of this method signature?
public static int add(int a, int b)
options: access modifier: public, keyword: static, return type: int, parameters: int a, int b
access modifier: static, keyword: public, return type: int, parameters: int a, int b
access modifier: public, keyword: static, return type: void, parameters: int a, int b
access modifier: int, keyword: static, return type: public, parameters: int a, int b
questions.answer: access modifier: public, keyword: static, return type: int, parameters: int a, int b

questions.question: What does the 'void' keyword indicate when used as a method's return type?
options: The method returns null
The method returns an empty String
The method does not return any value
The method returns 0
questions.answer: The method does not return any value

questions.question: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument in Java?
options: They are two names for the same thing
A parameter is the variable in the method definition; an argument is the value passed when calling the method
An argument is the variable in the method definition; a parameter is the value passed when calling the method
A parameter is used with void methods; an argument is used with methods that return values
questions.answer: A parameter is the variable in the method definition; an argument is the value passed when calling the method

options: A compilation error occurs

questions.question: How does Java handle parameter passing?
options: Pass-by-reference for all types
Pass-by-value for primitives and pass-by-reference for objects
Pass-by-value for all types
Pass-by-reference for primitives and pass-by-value for objects
questions.answer: Pass-by-value for all types

questions.question: When an array is passed to a method in Java, which statement is true?
options: A copy of the entire array is made, so modifications inside the method do not affect the original
A copy of the reference is passed, so modifications to the array elements are visible to the caller
The array is passed by reference, so reassigning the parameter changes the original variable
Arrays cannot be passed to methods in Java
questions.answer: A copy of the reference is passed, so modifications to the array elements are visible to the caller

questions.question: What happens when you reassign an array parameter to a new array inside a method?
options: The original array in the calling method is replaced with the new array
A compilation error occurs because arrays are final
The local copy of the reference points to the new array, but the original reference is unchanged
The original array is deleted from memory
questions.answer: The local copy of the reference points to the new array, but the original reference is unchanged

options: HELLO
hello
null
A compilation error occurs
questions.answer: hello

questions.question: Why must methods called from main be declared as static?
options: All methods in Java must be static
The main method is static, so it can only call other static methods directly without an object instance
Static methods run faster than non-static methods
The static keyword makes the method visible to other classes
questions.answer: The main method is static, so it can only call other static methods directly without an object instance

questions.question: Which method signature correctly defines a method that takes a double and returns a double?
options: public static double celsiusToFahrenheit(celsius) { ... }
public static double celsiusToFahrenheit(double celsius) { ... }
public static void celsiusToFahrenheit(double celsius) { ... }
public static int celsiusToFahrenheit(double celsius) { ... }
questions.answer: public static double celsiusToFahrenheit(double celsius) { ... }

questions.question: What does the return statement do in a Java method?
options: It prints the value to the console
It sends a value back to the code that called the method and exits the method
It stores the value in a global variable
It restarts the method from the beginning
questions.answer: It sends a value back to the code that called the method and exits the method

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid way to use a method that returns a value?
options: int result = square(5); and System.out.println(square(5)); are both valid
Only int result = square(5); is valid
Only System.out.println(square(5)); is valid
You must always store the return value in a variable before using it
questions.answer: int result = square(5); and System.out.println(square(5)); are both valid

options: true
false
questions.answer: false

questions.question: What is method composition?
options: Writing all logic inside a single large method
Methods calling other methods to build more complex behavior from simpler building blocks
Declaring multiple methods with the same name
Using inheritance to override a method
questions.answer: Methods calling other methods to build more complex behavior from simpler building blocks

options: false
true
A compilation error because isAlphanumeric cannot call isLetter
A runtime error
questions.answer: true

questions.question: Which best describes why breaking code into small methods is a best practice?
options: It makes the program run faster
It increases memory usage, which improves performance
It makes code more readable, testable, and reusable
It is required by the Java compiler
questions.answer: It makes code more readable, testable, and reusable

questions.question: Which return type is most appropriate for a factorial method to handle large values?
options: int
short
long
byte
questions.answer: long

questions.question: A method lcm(int a, int b) calls gcd(int a, int b) internally. What design principle does this demonstrate?
options: Encapsulation
Method composition — building complex methods from simpler ones
Polymorphism
Method overloading
questions.answer: Method composition — building complex methods from simpler ones

options: A compilation error occurs
The method name is invalid
The parameter type should be double
The method declares a return type of int but does not have a return statement
System.out.println cannot be used inside a method
questions.answer: The method declares a return type of int but does not have a return statement

questions.question: Which of the following is a best practice for method design?
options: A method should perform as many tasks as possible to reduce the number of methods
A method should do one thing, have a descriptive name, and have clear inputs and outputs
Method names should be as short as possible, preferably single letters
Methods should always return void to keep things simple
questions.answer: A method should do one thing, have a descriptive name, and have clear inputs and outputs

questions.question:
What value does sumOfDigits(1234) return if implemented correctly?

public static int sumOfDigits(int n) { ... }
Why does modifying a String parameter inside a method NOT affect the original String in the caller?
options: Strings are primitives in Java
Strings are passed by reference but are immutable, and reassignment only changes the local copy of the reference
Strings cannot be used as method parameters
The method creates a new thread that has its own copy of the String
questions.answer: Strings are passed by reference but are immutable, and reassignment only changes the local copy of the reference

questions.question: What is an Armstrong number?
options: A number that is divisible by all of its digits
A number where the sum of the cubes of its digits equals the number itself (e.g., 153 = 1^3 + 5^3 + 3^3)
A number that is both prime and even
A number whose digits are in ascending order
questions.answer: A number where the sum of the cubes of its digits equals the number itself (e.g., 153 = 1^3 + 5^3 + 3^3)
